name: The Armistice Resonance
description: Arlen felt the difference too. He's been in the lattice for seventeen years — he has been feeling the Armistice every night for seventeen years and he has been trying to find the language to describe it. He wants to try.
accept_text: Arlen's presence in the node chamber has the quality it gets when he has been thinking about something for a long time and has found someone he can finally say it to. 'I have been in the lattice during every Armistice for seventeen years,' he says. The resonance is warm and careful. 'I have been trying to find the right description for what I feel during those hours for most of that time.' A pause. 'When the guns stop — and I can feel that, at the node level, the specific reduction in discharge frequency is felt through the lattice as a kind of relief, the way you feel a loud sound stopping — when the guns stop, the lattice becomes easier to be in.' The crystal brightens slightly. 'That is not a metaphor. The lattice has a quality during Armistice hours that it does not have during the day. The integration at those hours is — more complete. The access between nodes is cleaner. I can feel Rund more clearly. I can feel the Ninth. The connections that exist all the time are — less obstructed.' A pause. 'I have not known what to make of this for seventeen years. Lena's instrument readings are — the first external evidence I have that what I feel is a real effect rather than my own preference.' The warmth. 'Will you take what I'm telling you to her? Tell her: the lattice recognizes the Armistice. Not as a political agreement. As a physical condition. The reduction in discharge, the absence of active crystalline detonation — the lattice responds to the absence of what hurts it.' A pause. 'Tell her: when the guns stop, the lattice can hear itself again. I don't know what that means for the second completion. But I think it is important.'
complete_text: Lena listens with the second notebook open, taking notes she almost never takes — she usually holds information in her head until she's back at the post. This time she writes while you speak. When you finish she reads what she's written back to herself slowly. 'The lattice recognizes the Armistice as a physical condition,' she says. She sets down the pen. 'The Armistice was formalized by two military commands as an operational arrangement. Nobody asked the lattice.' She pauses. 'But the lattice has been — responding to it. For three hundred years. Every night when the guns stop, the lattice becomes more coherent, more connected, the way Arlen describes.' She looks at the armistice ground. 'Three hundred years of nightly restoration.' Her voice is careful. 'The lattice has been healing, partially, every night during the Armistice for three hundred years. The damage the war does during the day is being partially repaired at night, during the hours of the ceasefire.' She is quiet for a long time. 'We did not design that. The Elder People did not build the Armistice. Nobody built the Armistice — it emerged.' She looks at you. 'Something that was not designed is partially healing something that was damaged. Every night. For three hundred years.' She writes one more line in the notebook and then closes it. 'I don't know what to do with this. I know it matters. I know it changes something about how I understand what I've been doing out here every night.' She looks at the ceasefire line. 'I came to the Armistice because I needed the rest and because Voss have been doing it since the founding and because it's the right thing professionally.' A pause. 'I stayed because of what it was. I didn't know what it was until now.' She picks up the notebook. 'I'm going to write this up and send it to Kira. She'll be able to see it from above. The lattice pattern she's been tracking — if the Armistice produces nightly restoration, there should be a daily rhythm in the pattern she's mapping from altitude.'
